title: Healthcare reps 'shocked' by 'unavoidable' comment after hospital violence
full_text:
The ACT government has been accused of denying healthcare workers occupational violence leave provisions in enterprise bargaining, with healthcare unions saying they are "shocked" by a minister's comments.
Health Minister Rachel Stephen-Smith said on Thursday, May 7, that dealing with upset patients and loved ones or "people who have little control over their own behaviour" was "unavoidable when you deliver a health service".
The remarks followed questions about an incident on April 22, in which eight emergency department staff members were held hostage in a glassed staffing area by a patient with a knife, and an alleged strangling assault on a nurse last year.
"Unfortunately our healthcare workers come in contact with people on some of the worst days of their lives, or sometimes people who have little control over their own behaviour ... and also come in contact with family members and parents who are extremely distressed by what is occurring," Ms Stephen-Smith said.
"People do get heightened in those situations. There is a lot of work that goes into saying to people that that is not acceptable, but that is the world in which we live."
In a statement, unions representing allied health workers and doctors said they were "shocked today [on Friday] after comments made by the Barr government in question time yesterday regarding occupational violence".
Healthcare staff should not have to accept violence in the workplace just because of their job, Australian Salaried Medical Officers Federation executive officer Tom Fischer said.
"Occupational violence is not an unavoidable part of being a doctor or any health worker," Mr Fischer said.
"Doctors make sacrifices every day to care for their patients and serve the communities they're a part of, but they should never be asked to sacrifice their own personal safety.
"[Canberra Health Services] and the ACT government are responsible for making sure this doesn't happen again."
Community and Public Service Union (CPSU) regional secretary Maddy Northam said "occupational violence in hospitals is not unavoidable".
"It is foreseeable, and this government needs to do more to manage this risk which starts with valuing and respecting hospital workers," Ms Northam said.
"CPSU members are deeply concerned that the [Andrew] Barr government isn't taking their safety at work seriously."
Unions representing healthcare workers are currently bargaining with the ACT government.
Nurses and midwives are trying to extend occupational leave to include critical incidents and trauma while negotiating the Public Sector Enterprise Agreement 2026.
Australian Nursing and Midwifery Federation ACT secretary Carlyn Fidow said:
"Not all occupational violence leaves a physical mark - witnessing a terrifying event, being trapped, or fearing for your safety can be just as damaging.
"Current leave provisions do not adequately reflect that reality, and we are demanding they be fixed."
Ms Northam, whose union represents many allied health workers and wardspeople (orderlies), claimed the government had agreed to include occupational leave in the next ACT public service enterprise agreement.
An ACT government spokesperson said the government "is committed to ensuring our workplaces are safe and our workforce is supported to undertake their roles and deliver services to the ACT community.
"[ACT public service] employees already have access to a variety of leave types in the event they experience occupational violence. This may include personal leave or the approval of 'Take leave where leave cannot be granted under any other provision' which does not come out of the employee's leave balance.
"Canberra Health Services (CHS) has a policy in place to support managers to provide this leave, and in response to the union claims for occupational violence leave, the government has committed to expanding this to a whole of government policy.
"Enterprise agreement bargaining is still underway and the government continues to negotiate in good faith."https://www.canberratimes.com.au/story/9239415/rachel-stephen-smith-responds-to-police-claims-canberra-hospital-knife-siege/
The doctors union, the Australian Salaried Medical Officers Federation, has applied to the Fair Work Commission to vote on a ballot to take action after 80 per cent of voting members rejected the ACT government's pay offer.
ACT President Professor Dip Talaulikar said "fatigued and overworked clinicians are finding it challenging to keep delivering high-quality patient care while training and meeting the professional development needs required to care for Canberrans".
"Uncompetitive working conditions and pay have led to chronic workforce shortages," Professor Talaulikar said.
summary: Healthcare unions in the ACT are expressing concern over comments made by Health Minister Rachel Stephen-Smith regarding occupational violence, arguing that such violence is not an unavoidable aspect of healthcare work.
author: Lanie Tindale
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: Minister Stephen-Smith stated that dealing with upset patients is 'unavoidable' in healthcare.
Unions are negotiating for better occupational violence leave provisions amid ongoing enterprise bargaining.
The Australian Salaried Medical Officers Federation is seeking to take action after rejecting the ACT government's pay offer.
relevance: This situation highlights the importance of workplace safety and employee welfare, which are critical considerations for actuaries involved in risk assessment and management within the healthcare sector.
